name: Short Film Shot List — Coverage Plan, Visualized

seo.title: Short Film Shot List Board
seo.description: A complete one-scene coverage plan: shot sizes with visual examples, a lettered shot list with lenses, the 180-degree rule, and movement notes — on one canvas.
seo.body: A working shot list for a two-person dialogue scene, built the way a director and DP would board it: the full shot-size ladder from extreme wide to extreme close-up with a visual example of each, a lettered coverage plan (master, over-the-shoulders, clean singles, inserts) with lens choices, and the craft notes that keep a set moving — the 180-degree rule, shooting wide to tight, and matching eyelines. Copy the structure, swap in your scene, and walk on set with a plan. Made with Soleil Clusters.

header.sub: One scene · full coverage · every setup planned before call time

images.query: film crew shooting behind the scenes

cards.title: Plan the day, then shoot the plan
cards.caption: A shot list is a schedule, a budget, and a style guide in one

header.sub: Learn the vocabulary once — every list is written in it

source.query: lone person vast landscape extreme wide

cards.title: EWS — Extreme Wide
cards.caption: Geography and scale; the character belongs to the world

source.query: full body person standing street wide shot

cards.title: WS — Wide
cards.caption: Full body; blocking and body language read clearly

source.query: waist up portrait person natural light
close up portrait face dramatic light

cards.title: CU — Close-Up
cards.caption: The face is the scene; save it for the beats that earn it

source.query: extreme close up human eye macro

cards.title: ECU — Extreme Close-Up
cards.caption: Detail as punctuation — an eye, a trigger, a ring

header.sub: Two actors, seven setups, half a day

rows.what: Master — whole scene, both actors in frame
Clean single — ANA, from the turn to the exit
Insert — the unsent message on the phone

cards.html:
Coverage logic
Shoot the master first while energy and light are fresh, then work wide to tight — makeup, continuity, and performance all degrade gracefully in that order. Inserts and cutaways go last; they forgive everything.

The 180° rule
Draw a line between your two actors and keep the camera on one side of it. Eyelines stay opposed, screen direction stays constant, and the audience always knows who is looking at whom.

header.sub: What the glass and the dolly are saying

cards.html:
Lens language
24mm — context and energy, space stretches.
35mm — how the eye sees a room.
50mm — honest, neutral, documentary.
85mm — intimacy and compression; backgrounds melt.

Move with a reason
Static — observation. Push-in — realization. Handheld — instability. Dolly — grace and inevitability. If the move has no motivation, lock it off.

cards.title: Motivated movement
cards.caption: The camera is a character with opinions
